section: Microstructure and mechanical properties of Fe–30Mn–9Al–C–3Ni low-density steel manufactured by selective laser melting

context_update.summary: This chunk comprises the Introduction section. It reviews the background of low-density Fe-Mn-Al-C steels doped with Ni, highlighting examples like Fe-16Mn-10Al-0.86C-5Ni (elongation 38%, tensile strength <1.5 GPa) and Fe-19Mn-9Al-0.8C-5Ni (yield strength >1.4 GPa after aging). It discusses limitations of traditional processing and introduces SLM as an alternative. Previous studies on SLM of AlSi10Mg, SS316L, René 104 with Sc, and Ti-6Al-4V are summarized to illustrate the effect of parameters and powder characteristics. The research gap is clearly stated: no SLM-fabricated Fe-Mn-Al-C alloy mechanical properties have been reported. The present study fills this gap by first preparing Fe-30Mn-9Al-C-3Ni powder via PREP and SLM printing at varying laser powers. The abstract reports that the sample produced at 110 W (P-110) achieves YS 887.1 MPa, UTS 1076.8 MPa, elongation 40.80%, and hardness 294 HV. Upcoming sections are expected to detail experimental methods, powder characterization, and the systematic effect of laser power on microstructure and properties. Key parameters to remember include laser powers (70, 90, 110, 130, 150 W) and SLM conditions (scanning speed 750 mm/s, layer thickness 0.02 mm, hatch spacing 0.08 mm).

Material systems: Fe-30Mn-9Al-C-3Ni; Fe-16Mn-10Al-0.86C-5Ni; Fe-19Mn-9Al-0.8C-5Ni; AlSi10Mg; SS316L; René 104 superalloy; Ti-6Al-4V
Sample aliases: P-110
Processing route: PREP powder production; SLM with laser powers 70-150 W; scanning speed 750 mm/s; layer thickness 0.02 mm; hatch spacing 0.08 mm; spot diameter 0.1 mm; substrate preheating 200 °C; argon atmosphere O₂<300 ppm
Key variables: laser power (70, 90, 110, 130, 150 W); scanning speed 750 mm/s; powder particle size 15-53 μm
Properties: yield strength 887.1 MPa; tensile strength 1076.8 MPa; elongation 40.80%; hardness 294 HV (P-110)

context_update.summary: This chunk covers Materials and methodology (2.1–2.3) and the Results for powder characterization (3.1) and alloy microstructure characterization (3.2). Fe-30Mn-9Al-C-3Ni powder prepared by PREP is spherical with D50 35.69 µm and contains γ-Fe(M) and minor α-Fe(M) phases. SLM samples were printed at laser powers 70, 90, 110, 130, 150 W (denoted P-70 to P-150) with 67° layer rotation. The printed alloy is fully austenitic; α-Fe is suppressed by fast cooling. With increasing laser power, FWHM of (111) decreases (0.289° to 0.237°), and average grain size increases (8.1 to 14.3 µm cross-section). Porosity first decreases (min 0.13% at P-110) then increases; relative density peaks at 99.34% for P-110. The P-110 sample exhibits the fewest microcracks and best surface quality. EBSD shows equiaxed grains in cross-sections and columnar grains in the longitudinal section (P-110 longitudinal grain size 14.9 µm). TEM reveals dislocation tangles and nanoscale κ carbides. Surface roughness decreases from 20.71 to 4.19 µm. The following sections are expected to present mechanical properties and fracture behavior for these samples, linking to the characterized microstructures.
microstructure: equiaxed grains in cross-section, random orientation
average grain size 8.1–14.3 μm cross-section, 14.9 μm longitudinal for P-110
mechanisms: higher energy density leads to slower cooling and larger grains
faster horizontal cooling causes smaller cross-section grains
excess laser power causes splashing, spheroidisation, internal stress, voids, cracks

Material systems: Fe-30Mn-9Al-C-3Ni; Fe-16Mn-10Al-0.86C-5Ni; Fe-19Mn-9Al-0.8C-5Ni; AlSi10Mg; SS316L; René 104 superalloy; Ti-6Al-4V; Fe-30Mn-9Al-C-3Ni powder
Sample aliases: P-110; P-70; P-90; P-130; P-150
Processing route: PREP powder production; SLM with laser powers 70-150 W; scanning speed 750 mm/s; layer thickness 0.02 mm; hatch spacing 0.08 mm; spot diameter 0.1 mm; substrate preheating 200 °C; argon atmosphere O₂<300 ppm
Key variables: laser power (70, 90, 110, 130, 150 W); scanning speed 750 mm/s; powder particle size 15-53 μm; laser power: 70, 90, 110, 130, 150 W; laser energy density: 58.33–125.00 J/mm³; layer thickness 0.02 mm; hatch spacing 0.08 mm; spot diameter 0.1 mm
Microstructure: equiaxed grains in cross-section, random orientation; columnar grains in longitudinal section; average grain size 8.1–14.3 μm cross-section, 14.9 μm longitudinal for P-110; κ carbide nanoparticles in austenite; no ferrite in SLM alloy; dislocation tangles
Properties: yield strength 887.1 MPa; tensile strength 1076.8 MPa; elongation 40.80%; hardness 294 HV (P-110); porosity min 0.13% (P-110); relative density max 99.34% (P-110); minimum density 6.78 g/cm³; surface roughness 20.71–4.19 μm
Mechanisms: higher energy density leads to slower cooling and larger grains; faster horizontal cooling causes smaller cross-section grains; excess laser power causes splashing, spheroidisation, internal stress, voids, cracks; 67° rotation reduces crystallographic texture; fast cooling suppresses ferrite formation
Figures or tables: Table 1; Fig. 1; Fig. 2; Fig. 3; Fig. 4; Fig. 5; Fig. 6; Fig. 7

context_update.summary: This chunk ends alloy characterization (noting surface spheroidization at 130–150 W) and reports comprehensive mechanical properties (Section 3.3). Tensile data show P-70 (YS 776.9 MPa, UTS 986.4 MPa, elongation 23.10%) is poor due to unfused powder; properties improve up to P-110 (YS 887.1 MPa, UTS 1076.8 MPa, elongation 40.80%), which outperforms cast alloy and conventional Fe-Mn-Al-C systems. Above 110 W, properties decline (P-150: YS 771.5 MPa, UTS 1032.0 MPa, elongation 28.28%). Fracture surfaces reveal ductile dimples in P-90/P-110 and defects (pores, cracks, spheroidization, unfused powder) in P-70, P-130, P-150. Hardness peaks at P-110 (292 HV cross-section, 294 HV longitudinal) and follows the same trend. The property trend is explained by competition between grain size (smaller at low power, larger at high power) and porosity. This chunk establishes the critical processing–structure–property relationships linking laser power, defects, grain size, and mechanical performance.
properties: P-70: YS 776.9 MPa, UTS 986.4 MPa, elongation 23.10%
P-110: YS 887.1 MPa, UTS 1076.8 MPa, elongation 40.80%, hardness 292 HV (cross), 294 HV (long)
P-150: YS 771.5 MPa, UTS 1032.0 MPa, elongation 28.28%
mechanisms: grain refining strengthening due to rapid cooling improves strength/plasticity vs cast
competition between grain size and porosity determines strength/plasticity trend with laser power
internal defects (pores, cracks, unfused powder) degrade mechanical properties

context_update.summary: This chunk first presents the hardness results: cross-sectional and longitudinal average hardnesses increase with laser power from 70 to 110 W, then decrease; P-110 achieves 292 HV cross-section, 294 HV longitudinal, with side surface softer than top surface. Both grain size and internal defects influence hardness. The chunk then provides the conclusions of the study: PREP powder is highly spherical, narrow-size, low-oxygen, good flowability, and free of hollow particles; all SLM samples contain austenite and nano κ carbides; laser power significantly affects microstructure and properties; P-110 delivers best combination (YS 887.1 MPa, UTS 1076.8 MPa, elongation 40.80%, hardness 294 HV). Tabulated tensile data (Table 2) compare all SLM conditions and as-cast. These conclusions summarize key findings needed for interpreting the overall processing–structure–property relationships in this alloy system.
properties: hardness 292 HV cross-section, 294 HV longitudinal at P-110
YS 887.1 MPa, UTS 1076.8 MPa, EL 40.80% at P-110
as-cast YS 772.3 MPa, TS 879.0 MPa, EL 20.53%
mechanisms: grain size and internal defects (pores, cracks, unfused powder) influence hardness

context_update.summary: This chunk delivers the complete tensile property dataset (Table 2) for all five SLM-printed and as-cast Fe–30Mn–9Al–C–3Ni samples, showing that the P-110 sample achieves the best combination with YS 887.1 MPa, UTS 1076.8 MPa, Z 35.16%, and EL 40.80%, while all SLM conditions surpass the as-cast reference. Fig. 14 presents average cross-sectional and longitudinal hardness values confirming that P-110 attains the highest hardness (292 HV cross-section, 294 HV longitudinal) and the hardness follows the same laser power trend as tensile properties. Fig. 13 a–e display fracture surface SEM images revealing evolution from lack-of-fusion and cracks (P-70, P-90) to ductile dimples (P-110), mixed dimples/cleavage (P-130), and cleavage/cracks/dimples (P-150), linking fracture characteristics to the processing–property relationship. No new composition or processing details appear. Key to remember: the optimal P-110 exhibits superior strength–ductility–hardness combination, and the fracture morphologies explain the property degradation at low and high laser powers.
microstructure: fracture surfaces: lack-of-fusion, cracks (P-70, P-90); dimples (P-110); dimples+cleavage (P-130); cleavage+cracks+dimples (P-150)
properties: as-cast YS 772.3 MPa, UTS 879.0 MPa, Z 17.10%, EL 20.53%
hardness trend with laser power (max at 110 W)
mechanisms: optimal P-110 shows ductile dimple fracture; low-power samples suffer from lack-of-fusion-induced early failure; high-power P-150 shows mixed cleavage and dimples due to defect formation and stress concentration

Summary: Tabulated tensile data (Table 2) compare all SLM conditions and as-cast. These conclusions summarize key findings needed for interpreting the overall processing–structure–property relationships in this alloy system. This chunk delivers the complete tensile property dataset (Table 2) for all five SLM-printed and as-cast Fe–30Mn–9Al–C–3Ni samples, showing that the P-110 sample achieves the best combination with YS 887.1 MPa, UTS 1076.8 MPa, Z 35.16%, and EL 40.80%, while all SLM conditions surpass the as-cast reference. Fig. 14 presents average cross-sectional and longitudinal hardness values confirming that P-110 attains the highest hardness (292 HV cross-section, 294 HV longitudinal) and the hardness follows the same laser power trend as tensile properties. Fig. 13 a–e display fracture surface SEM images revealing evolution from lack-of-fusion and cracks (P-70, P-90) to ductile dimples (P-110), mixed dimples/cleavage (P-130), and cleavage/cracks/dimples (P-150), linking fracture characteristics to the processing–property relationship. No new composition or processing details appear. Key to remember: the optimal P-110 exhibits superior strength–ductility–hardness combination, and the fracture morphologies explain the property degradation at low and high laser powers.
Material systems: Fe-30Mn-9Al-C-3Ni; Fe-16Mn-10Al-0.86C-5Ni; Fe-19Mn-9Al-0.8C-5Ni; AlSi10Mg; SS316L; René 104 superalloy; Ti-6Al-4V; Fe-30Mn-9Al-C-3Ni powder
Sample aliases: P-110; P-70; P-90; P-130; P-150; as-cast
Processing route: PREP powder production; SLM with laser powers 70-150 W; scanning speed 750 mm/s; layer thickness 0.02 mm; hatch spacing 0.08 mm; spot diameter 0.1 mm; substrate preheating 200 °C; argon atmosphere O₂<300 ppm
Key variables: laser power (70, 90, 110, 130, 150 W); scanning speed 750 mm/s; powder particle size 15-53 μm; laser power: 70, 90, 110, 130, 150 W; laser energy density: 58.33–125.00 J/mm³; layer thickness 0.02 mm; hatch spacing 0.08 mm; spot diameter 0.1 mm
Microstructure: equiaxed grains in cross-section, random orientation; columnar grains in longitudinal section; average grain size 8.1–14.3 μm cross-section, 14.9 μm longitudinal for P-110; κ carbide nanoparticles in austenite; no ferrite in SLM alloy; dislocation tangles; equiaxed and columnar grains; κ carbide nanoparticles
Properties: yield strength 887.1 MPa; tensile strength 1076.8 MPa; elongation 40.80%; hardness 294 HV (P-110); porosity min 0.13% (P-110); relative density max 99.34% (P-110); minimum density 6.78 g/cm³; surface roughness 20.71–4.19 μm
Mechanisms: higher energy density leads to slower cooling and larger grains; faster horizontal cooling causes smaller cross-section grains; excess laser power causes splashing, spheroidisation, internal stress, voids, cracks; 67° rotation reduces crystallographic texture; fast cooling suppresses ferrite formation; grain refining strengthening due to rapid cooling improves strength/plasticity vs cast; competition between grain size and porosity determines strength/plasticity trend with laser power; internal defects (pores, cracks, unfused powder) degrade mechanical properties
